content.method: Quasi-experimental decomposition using variation in local track availability, school-choice regulations, and settlement-level demographic data; difference-in-differences exploiting regional differences in track density combined with school-level segregation indices (dissimilarity/Gorard indices); triangulate with the disputed Horn/TARKI vs PISA-comparative statistics to reconcile methodological divergence.
content.question: What is the causal contribution of the 6/8-year gimnázium track structure itself—net of free school choice, residential sorting, church-school expansion, and selective commuting—to Hungary's EU-leading segregation levels?
content.why_it_matters: This is the foundational empirical question underlying every proposed reform option; without decomposing the causal channels, policymakers cannot know whether structural reform (T3), capacity contraction (T2/T6), or non-structural measures (T1/T4) will actually move the segregation outcome, or whether gains will simply be displaced.
